instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
Should You Choose A Rider When Buying A Term Plan?
Keywords: life insurance rider, insurance rider, waiver of premium
Meta Description: Wondering if you should purchase riders with your term plan? Read on to find out the different types of insurance riders and their benefits.
A term plan helps ensure the financial stability of your family members even in your absence. From meeting regular expenses to fulfilling important life goals like your children’s education, a term plan takes care of it all. By investing in a term plan, you essentially invest in safeguarding the financial future of your family.
While you can pick a tenure and coverage amount of your choice, term plans also come with certain additional insurance riders that can be purchased against a nominal increase to your premiums.
Should You Pick a Rider When Buying a Term Plan?
A term life insurance plan guarantees a sum assured payout only in the event of the policyholder’s death. Thus, opting for additional insurance riders with your term policy can help you remain prepared for different emergencies as well as keep you financially prepared to achieve your various life goals.
For instance, with an income benefit cover in place, your family receives both the sum assured as well as a regular income from your insurer. This helps guarantee that your children achieve their educational goals even after your untimely demise.
Common Riders that come with a Term Insurance Plan
If you’re looking to secure the financial future of your family with a term plan, opting for insurance riders that enhance the coverage umbrella of your plan may be prudent. Here’s a list of the most common riders that come with a term insurance plan:
1.   Critical Illness
While a critical illness (CI) diagnosis can be quite unnerving, opting for this add-on cover can help mitigate some of the financial burdens linked with such a diagnosis. With this rider, if you’re diagnosed with a critical illness -like kidney, liver or heart disease- you’ll be entitled to a predetermined lump-sum payout. You can use this sum to meet the high treatment costs associated with the condition. Moreover, since a CI diagnosis can cause a loss of income, you can also use this amount to support your family.
2.   Accidental Death
With the rising instances of accidents, you would want to ensure that your dependents are financially well-protected for the future. Opting for this life insurance rider ensures that your dependents receive not just the base sum assured from your term plan, but also an additional amount as an accidental death benefit. However, this additional benefit can only be availed if the death of the policyholder happens as a result of accidental death.
3.   Partial and Permanent Disability
If you sustain partial or permanent disabilities from an accident, your source of livelihood may be severely affected. In such cases, the partial and permanent disability insurance rider acts as your income replacement. Thus, your family will remain financially covered with the staggered payments from this rider even if you lose your source of income. You can use this amount to settle existing debts resulting from your disability or to meet your other life goals.
4.   Income Benefit
The income benefit rider ensures that in the event of your untimely demise, your family receives a regular income over and above the policy’s sum assured payout. Generally, this payment is made over a certain period, as a certain percentage of the sum assured value and can be used to meet the monthly expenses of your family.
Summing Up
While insurance riders help improve the protective cover of your term plan, it is important to understand their benefits before blindly opting for them. Since these riders come at an additional cost, it’s prudent to first assess your insurance requirements and life goals before purchasing them.

output.final_text:
Should You Choose A Rider while Buying A Term Plan?
Keywords: life insurance rider, insurance rider, waiver of premium
Meta Description: Wondering if you should purchase riders with your term plan? Read on to find out the different types of insurance riders and their benefits.
A term plan helps ensure the financial stability of your family members even in your absence. From meeting regular expenses to fulfilling important life goals like your children’s education, a term plan takes care of it all. By investing in a term plan, you essentially invest in safeguarding the financial future of your family.
While you can pick a tenure and coverage amount of your choice, term plans also come with certain additional insurance riders that can be purchased against a nominal increase to your premiums.
Should You Pick a Rider while Buying a Term Plan?
A term life insurance plan guarantees a sum assured payout only in the event of the policyholder’s death. Thus, opting for additional insurance riders with your term policy with nominal extra charges can help you remain prepared for different emergencies as well as keep you financially prepared to achieve your various life goals.
For instance, with an income benefit cover in place, your family receives both the sum assured as well as a regular income from your insurer. This helps to ensure that your children achieve their educational goals even after your untimely demise.
Common Riders that come with a Term Insurance Plan
If you’re looking to secure the financial future of your family with a term plan, opting for insurance riders that enhance the coverage umbrella of your plan may be prudent. Here’s a list of the most common riders that come with a term insurance plan:
1. Critical Illness
While a critical illness (CI) diagnosis can be quite unnerving, opting for this add-on cover can help mitigate some of the financial burdens linked with such a diagnosis. With this rider, if you’re diagnosed with a critical illness -as covered in the rider terms and conditions- you’ll be entitled to a
predetermined lump-sum payout. You can use this sum to meet the high treatment costs associated with the condition. Moreover, since a CI diagnosis can cause a loss of income, you can also use this amount to support your family financially. 2. Accidental Death With the rising instances of accidents, you would want to ensure that your dependents are financially well-protected for the future. Opting for this life insurance rider ensures that your nominees receive not just the base sum assured from your term plan, but also an additional amount as an accidental death benefit. However, this additional benefit can only be availed if the death of the policyholder happens as a result of accidental death.
3. Partial and Permanent Disability
If you sustain partial or permanent disabilities from an accident, your source of livelihood may be severely affected. In such cases, the partial and permanent disability life insurance rider acts as your income replacement. Thus, your family will remain financially covered with the staggered payments from this rider even if you lose your source of income. You can use this amount to settle existing debts resulting from your disability or to meet your other life goals.
4. Income Benefit
The income benefit rider ensures that in the event of your untimely demise, your nominee receives a regular income over and above the policy’s sum assured payout. Generally, this payment is made over a certain period depending upon policy terms and conditions of the rider, as a certain percentage of the sum assured value and can be used to meet the monthly expenses of your family.
Summing Up
While life insurance riders help increase the protective cover of your term plan, it is important to understand their benefits before blindly opting for them. Since these riders come at an additional cost, it’s prudent to first assess your insurance requirements and life goals before purchasing them.
